text:
In the production of soy sauce-aroma type baijiu (SSAB), the quality and safety of the final product are dictated by the characteristics of the base liquor. This study utilized headspace solid-phase microextraction gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (HS-SPME-GC-MS) alongside electronic nose (E-nose) technology to investigate the chemical markers associated with different quality grades in base liquors from the 3rd, 4th, and 5th fermentation rounds. 

Sensory analysis revealed that higher quality grades were positively associated with sauce, floral, fruity, and fermented aromas, as well as superior taste profiles (fullness, harmony, and persistence). The E-nose effectively distinguished between quality grades, showing higher response intensities for aromatics, sulfur compounds, and alcohols in superior-grade samples. Untargeted metabolomics identified 16 common differential compounds across all fermentation rounds, including 11 esters, 2 aldehydes, 2 ketones, and 1 alcohol. Principal component analysis (PCA) demonstrated that superior-grade liquors were characterized by a higher abundance of esters and alcohols, contributing to a more intense fruity profile. Furthermore, correlation analysis indicated that specific compounds, such as furfuryl ethyl ether, ethyl butanoate, and 3-methyl-1-butanol, significantly influenced E-nose sensor responses. These findings provide a digitized framework for quality grading and offer substantial guidance for optimizing the blending and aging processes in SSAB production.
